posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I see that there is a major fire in Landiras, which IIRC adjoins Sauternes. Does anybody have any info about this? Possible effects on Sauternes (and Barsac and Graves; maybe even Pessac-Leognan)?

Thank you in advance.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: James Guthri3
posts.cooked: Nothing to add about the impact on those wine regions, but I just read that French authorities suspect arson as the cause of that fire. Also saw that a third, smaller fire was reported in the Medoc. No details on the exact location yet.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
I see that there is a major fire in Landiras, which IIRC adjoins Sauternes. Does anybody have any info about this? Possible effects on Sauternes (and Barsac and Graves; maybe even Pessac-Leognan)?



Anyone else apart from me worried about smoke taint on these wines or are the fires are too far away for now?

The BBC has regularly updated reports on the heat and fire situation across Europe. From the latest report:

Gironde, a popular tourist region in the south-west, has been hit particularly badly, with firefighters from across France battling to control two blazes that have destroyed 19,300 hectares (47,700 acres) of land in the past week. The city of Bordeaux was blanketed by smoke early on Tuesday as the wind changed direction.

“It’s a monster like an octopus, and it’s growing and growing and growing in the front, in the back, on both sides,” said Jean-Luc Gleyze, Gironde’s regional president.

… A dozen animals at the zoo in La Teste-de-Buch [near Arcachon] died of stress and from the high temperatures, the government said. In a complex operation involving vets, zookeepers and others, 363 animals that could be moved were driven in convoys to Bordeaux-Pessac zoo 65km (40 miles) away.

The fire on the right is worryingly close to Sauternes:

Southern Gironde fires 2.JPG1041×724 141 KB

In the aerial photo, the smoke appears to be blowing west, however (counter to the normally prevailing winds off the Atlantic), so perhaps smoke damage is not yet a concern.
Landiras fire burn scar.JPG838×785 121 KB

Fire danger forecast.JPG830×625 91 KB

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Anyone else apart from me worried about smoke taint on these wines or are the fires are too far away for now?

Research so far shows that smoke taint is typically not a worry before veraison. It’s still way ahead of veraison in France.

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked:
Anyone else apart from me worried about smoke taint on these wines or are the fires are too far away for now?

Research so far shows that smoke taint is typically not a worry before veraison. It’s still way ahead of veraison in France.

This. While obviously worrisome for any scores of reasons, smoke taint is due to molecules within binding to sugar molecules. Pre-veraison this should likely not be problematic in the fruit and wine sense. There’s time to get the fires under control. Hopefully. Still, it is gut wrenching and I feel horribly for the people and homes and businesses and animals that are all directly impacted. Nasty business.

Nola Palomar posted today that she is harvesting in Spain.

posts.name: David Patte, Sun Break Wines
posts.cooked:
Anyone else apart from me worried about smoke taint on these wines or are the fires are too far away for now?

Research so far shows that smoke taint is typically not a worry before veraison. It’s still way ahead of veraison in France.

It that true this year?

Nola Palomar posted today that she is harvesting in Spain.

Also unfortunately, new research is showing risk regardless of berry development stages: “In the past it was believed that early-season smoke exposure posed a lower risk than exposure close to harvest; however, data from Australian smoke events in 2019/20 showed that there is a significant risk of perceptible smoke characters in wine, even when smoke exposure occurs prior to veraison.”. From: https://www.awri.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2012/04/smoke-taint-entry-into-grapes-and-vineyard-risk-factors.pdf

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It’s still way ahead of veraison in France.

No, véraison is beginning.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Research so far shows that smoke taint is typically not a worry before veraison. It’s still way ahead of veraison in France.

It that true this year?

Nola Palomar posted today that she is harvesting in Spain.

Also unfortunately, new research is showing risk regardless of berry development stages: “In the past it was believed that early-season smoke exposure posed a lower risk than exposure close to harvest; however, data from Australian smoke events in 2019/20 showed that there is a significant risk of perceptible smoke characters in wine, even when smoke exposure occurs prior to veraison.”. From: https://www.awri.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2012/04/smoke-taint-entry-into-grapes-and-vineyard-risk-factors.pdf

I hadn’t seen that yet David, thanks for posting it. That’s alarming at the least. Though anecodatally, we have a few vines of Cab Franc at Whistling Ridge that ripen later than the Pinot Noir and showed negligible smoke taint vs the Pinot Noir. The AWRI research is probably the better measure though.

Spain would be quite a bit ahead of France, but as William noted veraison is happening there already. Fingers crossed for everyone in Europe then, smoke issues are brutal and wine production is an enormous part of the economy over there.

posts.name: Roy Piper
posts.cooked: This reminds me a lot of Napa in 2020. It seems that other than Sauternes, the winds are mostly blowing south and to the west and away from the estates most Berserkers buy from. The first 2020 fire in Napa did that, and at the very same time of year as this. Hopefully more don’t break out. 104F at this early date without irrigation could be an issue if veraison has started. It can cause sunburn. The days of BDX being dry-farmed might be coming to an end soon. Between hail and heat and fires… it’s getting more difficult every year over there. I feel for them.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I’m in the Northern Médoc at the moment. The fire here was a few miles south at Vensac, next to Montalivet, so on the coast. 70 hectares were burnt but they got it under control quickly. We were pretty scared as we could see the smoke from our house.
The fire is a very long way from any vineyards so no danger there.
The other fires are much further south. The one behind the Dune de Pyla has now destroyed all five campsites there - which resonated a lot since I knew one of the former owners well. The other near Landiras could conceivably affect vineyards but so far apparently has not.
Both the big fires are now more or less under control but the whole region is like a tinderbox because of the lack of any rain for several weeks.
The good news is that temperatures are back to normal. Fingers crossed.

As I recall, driving through that area south of Bordeaux many years ago, there were a lot of pine forests. Is that right?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’m in the Northern Médoc at the moment. The fire here was a few miles south at Vensac, next to Montalivet, so on the coast. 70 hectares were burnt but they got it under control quickly. We were pretty scared as we could see the smoke from our house.

Both the big fires are now more or less under control but the whole region is like a tinderbox because of the lack of any rain for several weeks.
The good news is that temperatures are back to normal. Fingers crossed.

As I recall, driving through that area south of Bordeaux many years ago, there were a lot of pine forests. Is that right?

Yes, and they have seen big fires in the past, notably in 1989 and 1949.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
From the tip of the Médoc all the way down to just north of Tarnos, not far from Bayonne, there are vast pine forests, planted in the 19th century I think, to combat and dry out what was mostly marshland, very poor and rife with disease.
Fires are actually quite rare because the forests are well-organised with large tree-free corridors and strict maintenance regulations, along with manned surveillance towers. I suspect that the regulations were less closely respected and the surveillance less efficient, hence the fires, of course exacerbated greatly by the dry weather.
I think everyone was so spooked by the fires that they will be extra careful to watch out for any new ones but it was quite scary to see the billowing clouds of smoke only a few miles away.
Sadly for us, although it’s a very minor inconvenience in the circumstances, all the wonderful cycle paths through the forests are closed until it rains again.

posts.name: Richard T r i m p i
posts.cooked:
From the tip of the Médoc all the way down to just north of Tarnos, not far from Bayonne, there are vast pine forests, planted in the 19th century I think, to combat and dry out what was mostly marshland, very poor and rife with disease.
Fires are actually quite rare because the forests are well-organised with large tree-free corridors and strict maintenance regulations, along with manned surveillance towers.

It makes sense to battle out-of-control blazes but it does seem like there’re lessons from the American West that might prove useful…if unpopular in France. As dry heat waves are likely to be more frequent, years of aggressive fire suppression can lead to catastrophically large fires. It might make sense to adopt more active prescribed burns. Longleaf pine forest management in the Southern US includes it. Without fairly regular burn cycles, the pine litter and woody debris accumulate and the fires become too hot, killing many of the mature trees that would survive a faster and less intense burn.

RT
posts.display_username: Richard T r i m p i

posts.name: Nola.Palomar
posts.cooked:
Spain would be quite a bit ahead of France, but as William noted veraison is happening there already. Fingers crossed for everyone in Europe then, smoke issues are brutal and wine production is an enormous part of the economy over there.

I realized that, but the fact that she was harvesting now suggested that everything was well ahead of a normal vintage.

Our normal harvest is end of July to early August. Nothing to do with smoke or the fires in Spain or France. Obviously, it’s not every varietal that is ready right now. But we are about 10 days ahead of normal harvest.

posts.name: John Oglesby
posts.cooked:
Anyone else apart from me worried about smoke taint on these wines or are the fires are too far away for now?

Research so far shows that smoke taint is typically not a worry before veraison. It’s still way ahead of veraison in France.

I believe we learned in 2008 that smoke taint can happen pre-veraison. Anderson Valley was smoked out in June that year.

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked:
I’m in the Languedoc right now (a week of wine tasting coordinated by the lovely New Orleans-based wine shop The Independent). A fire broke out Thursday afternoon near where we were tasting in the area of Limoux. That fire contributed to a freak thunderstorm that broke out in the area that helped extinguish the blaze overnight.

I was staying in the La Clape area, which is to the east. Around 6pm Friday I took a nap, no smoke and no prospect of rain. I awoke from my nap a bit later and there was a huge storm, and then wind was sucking the smoke under the storm, out to sea. It was quite surreal.

I say freak storm because when the fires broke out there was a zero percent chance of precipitation for the rest of the day. The fires started at the east side of the storm and the interaction between the fires, wind and sea kicked up the storm quite a bit; it lasted 5 hours, and stayed camped out in that area pretty much the entire time. Which was helpful as it’s been insanely hot and dry here for weeks.

Then on the train from Narbonne to Carcassonne yesterday another fire broke out, but they put that one out in a couple hours.

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked: And can confirm, veraison is well under way in the Languedoc. Harvest starting in the next couple weeks.
